rationale: Em-dash (U+2014) over-use is a FREQUENCY tell, not a per-occurrence one. 2026 detection research (Pangram; Wikipedia 'Signs of AI writing') is explicit that a single em-dash proves nothing — humans use them as a deliberate stylistic device — and that the signal only appears when LLMs sprinkle them formulaically throughout a text. Each occurrence is recorded (tier-3, never surfaced as an individual finding) but the SCORE now responds to em-dash DENSITY: once occurrences per 100 prose words cross a floor, the Density dimension is penalised proportionally. A handful of intentional em-dashes costs nothing; a text peppered with them is dragged down. This is the 'soft' frequency check the per-occurrence model could not express.

Generic tricolon (rule of three: 'A, B and C') is a soft stylistic tell, but a rhetorical rule-of-three is not reliably distinguishable from an ordinary three-item enumeration ('Python, JavaScript and TypeScript') with surface heuristics. It is therefore NOT surfaced and emits no individual finding. The HIGH-confidence sub-variants of the rule of three ARE detected separately and surfaced: adjective tricola after a dash/colon and bare three-adjective segments (struct_adj_tricolon), and repeated sentence openers (struct_anaphora). Those two avoid the enumeration false-positive because a real list ('iPhone, iPad and Android') is neither a clause-final adjective triple nor an anaphora.

Negative parallelism ('nicht nur … sondern auch …' / 'not just … but (also) …' / 'not only … but also …') is a rhetorical template heavily over-used in LLM output to signal breadth or contrast. Human writers use it occasionally; in AI text it appears as a reflex on nearly every second claim. Tier-2, surfaced only inside a tier-2 CLUSTER (≥3 tier-2 findings in a 10-line window) — a single contrast is legitimate rhetoric, a cluster is the tell.

Anaphora — consecutive sentences/fragments that open with the same word — is one of the clearest modern AI MARKETING tells. The canonical form is the 'No X. No Y. Just Z.' / 'Kein X. Kein Y. Nur Z.' staccato (2026 sources: Wikipedia 'Signs of AI writing'; oliviacal AI-tells; contentconsultants 'Antithesen/Stakkato'). It is detected per prose segment: a run of ≥3 consecutive sentences sharing an opener, OR a run of ≥2 consecutive sentences opening with a NEGATION (kein*/nicht/no/not), fires one finding. Unlike generic tricolon this does NOT collide with ordinary enumerations: 'Python, JavaScript and TypeScript' is one sentence, not three with a shared opener. Tier-2 and ALWAYS surfaced (high confidence), bypassing the tier-2 cluster gate.

The clause-final three-adjective burst — 'groß, klar, motivierend', 'einfach, visuell, motivierend', 'fast, clean, reliable' — is a high-confidence rule-of-three variant. A 2026 German source (contentconsultants) names '3 kommagetrennte Adjektive nach Gedankenstrich' explicitly as a tell. Detected in two tight shapes: (1) a dash/colon followed by exactly three single-word items at the clause end, and (2) a whole short segment that is nothing but three comma-separated single-word items. Both shapes exclude real enumerations, which are normally embedded in a longer phrase ('verfügbar für iPhone, iPad und Android — …') rather than standing alone as a clause-final adjective triple. Tier-2 and ALWAYS surfaced, bypassing the tier-2 cluster gate.
